past_medical_history: Epilepsy diagnosed at age 21, managed with levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily
History of syncopal episodes approximately 10 years ago following first pregnancy, attributed to vasovagal etiology at that time
Gravida 3, para 1, currently at 33 weeks gestation

medications.indication: Neural tube defect prevention (on antiepileptic drug)

family_history: Mother with history of seizures, untreated
No family history of neurodegenerative disease or brain tumors

social_history.occupation: Previously worked in agriculture in Guatemala, currently unemployed
social_history.living_situation: Currently residing at a homeless shelter with her 10-year-old son
social_history.travel: Emigrated from rural Guatemala 2 years ago, grew up on a farm with pigs and cattle

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent in Spanish with interpreter assistance; no paraphasic errors noted. Able to follow complex commands. Memory intact for recent and remote events. GCS 15. No signs of confusion or post-ictal state at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities bilaterally. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing performed without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady with mildly widened base attributed to gravid habitus. Tandem gait not formally tested due to pregnancy. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue bite observed. No signs of head trauma. Fundoscopic exam without papilledema.

patient.chief_complaint: Syncopal episode with witnessed loss of consciousness and suspected seizure at homeless shelter
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Ramirez is a 31-year-old right-handed Hispanic woman at 33 weeks of gestation who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed syncopal episode at a homeless shelter. Shelter staff report that the patient was sitting at a table when she suddenly stopped talking and appeared to stare blankly for approximately 10 seconds. She then developed stiffening of her upper extremities followed by rhythmic jerking of all four limbs lasting approximately 60 seconds. She was placed on her left side by shelter staff during the episode. Afterward, she was confused and unable to answer questions for approximately 20 minutes before gradually returning to her baseline. Upon interview through a Spanish interpreter, the patient recalls experiencing a strong sense of familiarity, as if she had lived through that moment before, accompanied by a rising sensation in her stomach just before losing awareness. She reports a history of similar brief episodes of intense deja vu with an epigastric rising sensation occurring two to three times per month over the past several years, each lasting approximately 30 to 60 seconds, after which she feels briefly disoriented. She was diagnosed with epilepsy approximately 10 years ago following the birth of her first child and was started on levetiracetam at that time. She reports inconsistent access to her medication since arriving in the United States and estimates she has missed several doses over the past two weeks due to difficulty obtaining refills. She also reports a headache that began approximately two hours after the event, described as bilateral, dull, and rated 4 out of 10 in severity. She denies nausea, vomiting, neck stiffness, fever, chills, visual changes, weight loss, or any new focal weakness. She denies any recent head trauma, illness, or changes in medications other than the missed doses. She reports no complications with her current pregnancy and states her prenatal care has been followed at a community health clinic. She experienced similar syncopal episodes after the birth of her first child approximately 10 years ago in Guatemala, which prompted her initial epilepsy diagnosis.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm attenuating with eye opening
background.sleep_features: Sleep architecture preserved with vertex waves and sleep spindles during stage II sleep

findings.frequency: Frequent, occurring every 3-4 minutes during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness and sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with field extending to T4 and F4, duration 40-60 ms, followed by after-going slow wave

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta activity lasting 2-6 seconds

artifacts.impact: Minimal, did not obscure interictal abnormalities

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to frequent right anterior temporal sharp waves with phase reversal at F8 and intermittent right temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). No electrographic seizures were captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to pregnancy. Routine 40-minute recording; no clinical events captured. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring recommended to capture habitual events.

findings.location: Scattered throughout bilateral cerebral white matter, right thalamus, and bilateral basal ganglia
findings.size: Each measuring 2-5 mm in diameter, approximately 12-15 total lesions identified

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No surrounding hyperintense signal or edema around any of the calcifications
signal_characteristics.SWAN_SWI: Multiple rounded hypointense areas confirming calcified nature of the lesions

findings.borders: Well-defined rounded margins, no surrounding edema

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume mildly reduced compared to left

signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintense signal in right hippocampus relative to left
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Mild hyperintense signal in right hippocampal body

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.6 cm3 (below 10th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No contrast administered due to pregnancy
Ventricular system normal in size and configuration
No evidence of active cystic lesions or ring-enhancing lesions (limited assessment without contrast)
mri.impression: No surrounding edema or active cystic lesions identified. Additionally, subtle right hippocampal volume loss with mild T2/FLAIR signal abnormality raises concern for early right mesial temporal sclerosis, possibly secondary to chronic epileptogenic focus related to the calcified lesions. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with slight sinus tachycardia appropriate for third trimester of pregnancy. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes identified.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture observed over 48 hours of continuous monitoring

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 10 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 20 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximum negativity at F8/T4, followed by after-going slow wave
findings.state: All states of consciousness, maximal during NREM sleep

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric
findings.frequency: One clinical seizure captured over 48 hours
findings.morphology: Right temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta activity evolving to 2-3 Hz delta with spread to right parasagittal region over 50 seconds, then bilateral involvement
findings.state: Seizure occurred during wakefulness while patient was seated in bed

findings.morphology: Rhythmic 2-3 Hz delta activity in right temporal chain

output.impression: Abnormal 48-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. One habitual clinical seizure captured with clear right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4) concordant with clinical semiology of temporal lobe seizures. Frequent right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges and TIRDA. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to pregnancy. 48-hour monitoring, single seizure captured.

findings.location: Bilateral cerebral white matter, right thalamus, bilateral basal ganglia

signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Hypointense foci on thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: No perilesional FLAIR hyperintensity, indicating quiescent calcified stage
signal_characteristics.SWAN_SWI: Confirmed calcified nature of all lesions

findings.location: Right hippocampus, body and tail segments
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume 2.4 cm3, left 3.3 cm3, asymmetry index 0.16

signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Hyperintense signal in right hippocampus on 3 mm thin coronal cuts perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in right hippocampal body and tail
signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Loss of internal architecture of the right hippocampus

findings.mass_effect: Mild right temporal horn prominence consistent with ex vacuo dilation
findings.borders: Well-defined atrophic changes without associated mass

findings.type: Calcified granuloma near right temporal neocortex
findings.location: Right inferior temporal gyrus, subcortical white matter

additional_observations: No evidence of active cystic or enhancing lesions
No focal cortical dysplasia identified elsewhere
output.impression: A calcified granuloma in the right inferior temporal gyrus subcortical white matter is likely a calcified neurocysticercosis lesion and may represent the inciting lesion for the ipsilateral mesial temporal sclerosis. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36 T-score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 T-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Visual memory learning (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36
Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32

findings.location: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, and amygdala) and moderately reduced uptake in the right lateral temporal neocortex compared to the left
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.76 (normal >0.90), indicating significant right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired post-partum during EEG-confirmed interictal state
No areas of hypermetabolism to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. Multi-modal concordance supports right temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: neurocysticercosis temporal lobe epilepsy calcified lesions management pregnancy

results.title: Neurocysticercosis as a cause of epilepsy and seizures in endemic regions
results.source: Garcia HH et al., Lancet Neurol 2014;13(12):1202-1215
results.summary: Neurocysticercosis is the most common cause of acquired epilepsy worldwide. Calcified lesions (involutional stage) can serve as epileptogenic foci and are associated with perilesional gliosis leading to mesial temporal sclerosis. Serology may be negative in up to 50% of cases with only calcified lesions.

results.title: Management of epilepsy in pregnancy: current evidence
results.source: Tomson T et al., Lancet Neurol 2019;18(2):164-176
results.summary: Levetiracetam is considered one of the safer AEDs in pregnancy. Clearance increases 40-60% during pregnancy, requiring dose adjustments. Monthly therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended. Seizure freedom should be maintained to protect both mother and fetus.

results.title: Sensitivity of serological tests in neurocysticercosis by disease stage
results.source: Wilson M et al., Clin Infect Dis 2009;49(7):e70-e75
results.summary: The EITB (enzyme-linked immunoelectrotransfer blot) has 98% specificity but sensitivity drops to approximately 50-70% in patients with only calcified granulomas. Negative serology does not exclude the diagnosis when imaging is characteristic.

output.summary: Neurocysticercosis is the leading cause of acquired epilepsy in endemic regions. Calcified lesions can cause chronic epilepsy through perilesional gliosis and secondary mesial temporal sclerosis. Serological sensitivity is reduced in the calcified stage. In pregnant patients with epilepsy, levetiracetam is preferred, with dose adjustments for increased clearance. Monthly drug level monitoring is recommended throughout pregnancy.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily (dose increase for pregnancy-related clearance)
interactions: No significant interactions with prenatal vitamins or folic acid
warnings: Levetiracetam clearance increases 40-60% during pregnancy; monthly drug level monitoring recommended
Dose should be reduced to pre-pregnancy levels in early postpartum period to avoid toxicity
Psychiatric adverse effects (irritability, mood changes) in approximately 10-15% of patients
Breast milk excretion occurs but generally considered compatible with breastfeeding
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine (also pregnancy-safe, but clearance increases 50-100% and requires more frequent monitoring)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily (acceptable in pregnancy but less data than levetiracetam)
Avoid valproic acid - teratogenic, absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right temporal lobe epilepsy secondary to calcified neurocysticercosis with secondary mesial temporal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: Obstetrical causes excluded by Ob/Gyn evaluation. Witnessed seizure semiology with tonic-clonic activity lasting >60 seconds and prolonged post-ictal confusion inconsistent with convulsive syncope. Recurrent hypotension during hospitalization responded to IV fluids but was a separate finding.
Classic temporal lobe aura (deja vu, epigastric rising), witnessed motor automatisms, and clear EEG correlate with epileptiform discharges and TIRDA argue strongly against PNES.
Blood pressure normal, no proteinuria, no edema, no headache with visual changes. Obstetrical evaluation unremarkable. Prior history of seizures with known structural etiology.
Pattern of calcifications on imaging more consistent with NCC than toxoplasmosis. Clinical context (rural Central American origin, farm exposure) strongly favors NCC. No immunocompromise.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed seizure history including semiology, aura description, witness account, medication adherence, and duration of post-ictal period
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Classic temporal lobe aura (deja vu, epigastric rising) with focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure and post-ictal confusion. Subtherapeutic AED due to missed doses.
Normal interictal neurological examination without focal deficits

optimal_actions.action: Order EEG to characterize epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right anterior temporal sharp waves and TIRDA consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to identify structural etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple calcified neurocysticercosis lesions and right hippocampal sclerosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, levetiracetam level, and infectious workup
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level, normal metabolic panel, negative cysticercosis serology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to exclude cardiac arrhythmia as cause of syncopal episode
Optimize levetiracetam dose to achieve therapeutic levels, accounting for pregnancy-related increased clearance
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions; dose increase to 750 mg twice daily recommended with monthly monitoring

optimal_actions.action: Obstetrical consultation to exclude pregnancy-related causes of syncope and monitor fetal well-being
Plan post-partum contrast-enhanced MRI for definitive characterization of calcified lesions and consider referral to comprehensive epilepsy center
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral placed for post-partum imaging and epilepsy surgery evaluation

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to characterize and lateralize epileptiform activity
Obtain brain MRI to identify structural etiology (neurocysticercosis calcifications and mesial temporal sclerosis)
Check levetiracetam level and adjust dose for pregnancy-related increased clearance
Exclude obstetrical and pregnancy-related causes of seizure (preeclampsia/eclampsia)
Ensure seizure safety precautions and fetal monitoring during hospitalization
Recognize that negative cysticercosis serology does not exclude the diagnosis in calcified-stage disease
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing valproic acid, which is teratogenic and absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy
Performing lumbar puncture without clear indication (no signs of meningitis or raised intracranial pressure)
Administering gadolinium contrast for MRI during pregnancy without compelling indication
Discharging patient without optimizing antiepileptic drug therapy and confirming medication access
Initiating antihelminthic therapy (albendazole/praziquantel) for calcified-stage NCC, which is not indicated and could provoke perilesional edema
key_reasoning_points: The combination of deja vu and epigastric rising sensation is a classic temporal lobe aura, strongly localizing seizure onset to the mesial temporal structures

Multiple scattered cerebral calcifications in a patient from a neurocysticercosis-endemic region (rural Guatemala with farm exposure) are characteristic of calcified/involutional stage NCC, even with negative serology
Negative cysticercosis IgG does not exclude the diagnosis, as serological sensitivity drops to 50-70% in patients with only calcified granulomas
Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level (5.8 mcg/mL) due to medication non-adherence and pregnancy-related increased clearance is the likely precipitant for the breakthrough seizure
Right hippocampal sclerosis may represent secondary mesial temporal sclerosis caused by chronic seizure activity originating from a nearby calcified NCC granuloma
Pregnancy complicates management: increased AED clearance requires dose adjustment, gadolinium contrast is avoided, and eclampsia must be excluded as an alternative seizure etiology
Concordance across modalities (temporal lobe semiology, right temporal EEG focus, right hippocampal sclerosis on MRI) confirms the epileptogenic zone localization

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic temporal lobe seizure semiology, clear EEG lateralization, and characteristic imaging findings of neurocysticercosis. The primary diagnostic challenge is recognizing NCC as the underlying etiology despite negative serology, but the imaging pattern is unmistakable in the clinical context.
